text: The surgeon performs a fundoplication procedure using a Nathanson liver retractor to elevate the liver, providing clear access to the surgical site.
The surgeon begins separating approximately ten centimeters of the short gastric vessels from the fundus using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon uses the left hand to retract the stomach upwards while the right hand operates a harmonic scalpel to separate the short gastric vessels from the stomach fundus.
The surgeon lifts the stomach in a manner resembling a loaf of bread to avoid proximity to the spleen, facilitating easier separation of the short gastric vessels.
The surgeon uses an ethyl bond suture to perform a 360-degree wrap of the stomach around the esophagus, completing the Nissen fundoplication.
The surgeon uses the left hand to apply anterior traction on the stomach while the right hand operates a harmonic device to separate the short gastric vessels from the fundus.
The assistant holds the short gastric vessels while the surgeon uses a harmonic device to separate them from surrounding tissues.

The surgical team utilizes five ports for the procedure: an umbilical port located five centimeters above the umbilicus, ports in the left and right hypochondrium, a port in the left anterior axillary line, and an epigastric port for the tensile liver retractor.

The surgeon uses a harmonic ligature to manipulate the short gastric vessels, approaching the spleen. Anteromedial traction is applied to carefully separate any adhesions between the spleen and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues to separate the short gastric vessels, applying anterior traction to facilitate the dissection process.

The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum over the left crus, revealing the diaphragm. The spleen is visible on the left side, and further mobilization is performed to separate the peritoneum from the spleen.
The surgeon completes the peritoneal dissection on the left side, reaching up to the left crus. The spleen is positioned post-laterally, ensuring a clear dissection plane.
The surgeon begins anterior mobilization of the stomach, progressing towards the pars flaccida, using laparoscopic instruments through multiple ports.
The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum over the left crus, applying proper traction to ensure that only the peritoneum is separated without injuring the adjacent esophagus.
The surgeon performs left side mobilization on the posterior aspect, ensuring proper fundal mobilization necessary for the wrap in the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the anterior oesophagus, working over the left crus with visible dissection of the peritoneum.

The surgeon applies anterolateral traction to open the peritoneum over the pars flaccida, revealing the caudate lobe of the liver beneath. As the transparent peritoneum is mobilized, the anterior vagus nerve embedded in the area is carefully preserved during the anterior mobilization of the peritoneum over the esophagus.

The surgeon identifies the right crus and carefully separates the peritoneum over it, pushing the esophagus towards the left. The esophagus is mobilized from the right crus to create a posterior window.

The surgeon observes a good amount of intra-abdominal esophagus, approximately three to five centimeters, reducing the need for further esophageal mobilization from the mediastinum. The posterior window is formed, and the vagus nerve is visible alongside the esophagus. The posterior window is enlarged, confirming sufficient esophageal mobilization.
The posterior window is visible, showing the fundus of the stomach through it. The oesophagus is sufficiently present in the abdomen, eliminating the need for crural approximation.

The surgeon prepares to wrap the stomach around the oesophagus using a semi-traumatic grasper introduced from the right side. The stomach is pulled from left to right, creating a wrap with a floppy fundus visible behind the oesophagus. The wrap forms a 'buttock sign,' resembling a child in the prone position.

The surgeon forms a buttock sign with the stomach, ensuring the two 'buttocks' are equal in size. The stomach is pulled into position for suturing, and a 360-degree wrap is created around the esophagus using non-absorbable sutures, such as Ethibond.

The surgeon uses a curved needle to take a full-thickness bite in the stomach, starting from the left side and moving to the right. This is followed by a reverse bite on the opposite end of the stomach.

The surgeon ties a surgeon's knot using a suture, starting with a double wrap followed by two alternating wraps in opposite directions. The tail of the suture is visible as the first knot is tightened.
The surgeon ties the first knot, followed by the second knot, and then completes the last knot using a two-one-one technique with a double wrap initially, followed by two opposite alternating wraps.
The surgeon completes the wrapping of the stomach around the esophagus and proceeds to cut the suture with scissors, finalizing the Nissen fundoplication.

The surgeon ties the second knot approximately two centimeters away from the first knot, taking a full-thickness bite of the stomach. A vein of the gastric is encountered, causing slight bleeding, which stops once the knot is tightened.
The surgeon completes the Nissen fundoplication, ensuring the knot does not include the esophagus, marking the end of the procedure.

The surgeon cuts the suture and safely removes the needle. The Nathanson liver retractor is removed, allowing the liver to drop back into place. The gas is evacuated from the abdominal cavity, and the surgical wound is closed.
